text: Following diagnostic laparoscopy confirming no disseminated metastatic disease, the surgeon lyses previous operative adhesions to facilitate additional port placement.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative ultrasound on the liver to visually confirm the absence of additional metastatic disease.
The surgeon uses cautery to score the surface of the liver, marking the boundaries for the resection of the solitary metastasis in segment three.
During the scoring of the liver surface, the surgeon discovers an adherent tumor focus on the distal anterior gastric wall.
The surgeon excises a portion of the stomach en bloc using a harmonic scalpel, addressing concerns of metastatic invasion.
The surgeon excises a lesion at the distal location due to concerns of luminal narrowing, opting for direct excision rather than using a stapled resection with an endo-GIA device.
The surgeon mobilizes the left lobe of the liver, elevating it to score the posterior boundaries of the liver resection using cautery.
Following the marking of resection boundaries, the surgeon increases the cautery energy and begins a deeper division of the hepatic parenchyma, visibly cutting through the liver tissue.
The surgeon approaches the liver from the left lateral side for initial dissection, navigating around midline postoperative adhesions and adjusting for the angulation of the laparoscopic ports.
The surgeon uses an endo-GIA stapler with 3.5 mm staples to transect the thin lateral substance of the liver, facilitating parenchymal division.
The surgeon applies gentle opposing traction while using cautery to perform deeper dissection into the hepatic parenchyma, further advancing the liver resection.
The surgeon advances deeper into the liver segment using a harmonic scalpel, which provides greater control over the parenchymal division and seals larger vessels encountered during the dissection.

The surgeon encounters small peripheral vessels within the hepatic parenchyma, carefully dissecting them free from surrounding tissue and applying laparoscopic clips proximally and distally to secure them.
The surgeon divides the vessels and continues parenchymal transection using a technique similar to the open finger fracture method, visibly separating the liver tissue.
The surgeon applies vascular clips to hepatic vessels to control minor bleeding, enabling the continuation of parenchymal transection.
The surgeon encounters larger segmental vessels deep within the hepatic parenchyma, carefully dissecting them free from the surrounding tissue.
After dividing the bulk of the surrounding hepatic parenchyma, the surgeon uses an endo-GIA stapler with 2.5mm vascular staples to safely transect larger segmental vessels.
The surgeon continues dissection at the lateral edge of the falciform ligament using a harmonic scalpel, progressing until encountering another large segmental vessel within the hepatic parenchyma.
The surgeon thins the surrounding hepatic parenchyma, preparing the area for the application of an endoscopic stapler to divide the vascular structures.
The surgeon completes the division of the final vessel, fully separating the liver specimen from the remaining hepatic tissue.

The surgeon uses high-energy cautery to coagulate the remaining raw surface of the liver, achieving hemostasis with minimal oozing. In specific areas, Surgicel is applied alongside cautery to further control bleeding.
The surgeon closes the remaining gastric defect transversely using a two-layer closure technique, ensuring the distal stomach is not narrowed.
The surgeon applies a running 2-0 Vicryl suture to the inner layer of the closure, followed by interrupted 2-0 silk sutures on the outer layer, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon places the resected liver specimen into a large endobag and removes it through an enlarged port site incision.
